topic.prompt_en: Explain whether you agree, disagree, or partly agree with an idea.
topic.learning_goal: Learn to handle agreement questions with a clear position and visible nuance.

angles.title: State your position
content: Answer the question directly at the start.
A clear position gives the rest of the answer direction.

angles.title: Give the main reason
content: Choose one or two reasons that carry the answer.
Do not pile up weak points just to sound complete.

angles.title: Acknowledge the other side
content: Show what the alternative can offer.
This makes the answer sound more mature and realistic.

angles.title: End with a balanced conclusion
content: Finish with the condition, limit, or context that matters.
That keeps the answer flexible rather than extreme.

sequence.target: State your main view, preference, or comparison clearly.
Support your point and mention the alternative side.
End with the context where your view may change.

high_score_tips: A balanced opinion sounds stronger than a dramatic or rigid statement.
Give one concrete example if the topic risks becoming too abstract.
Use flexible language like generally, in my experience, or in some cases.
Your closing sentence should show when your opinion applies and when it might not.

content.problem: Taking an extreme position too quickly
content.why_it_hurts: The answer sounds less thoughtful if there is no room for context or exceptions.
content.fix: Keep your main view clear, but acknowledge the conditions around it.

content.why_it_hurts: A comparison is weak if the listener does not know what standard you are using.
content.fix: Compare cost, time, comfort, freedom, or long-term effect explicitly.

content.why_it_hurts: Some topics need nuance, and a fully certain answer can sound unrealistic.
content.fix: Use phrases like to some extent, in most cases, or it depends on.

content.why_it_hurts: The response sounds memorised if it stays at the level of general ideas and never lands on one lived detail.
content.fix: Add one concrete moment, reaction, or small consequence that actually happened to you.

content.problem: Reflection that only repeats the opening
content.why_it_hurts: The ending feels flat if it only restates the topic instead of adding insight or change.
content.fix: Use the final lines to show what changed, what you realised, or how you would respond now.

language.wrong_example: I am agree with this idea.
language.reason: Agree is a verb, so it does not take am.

language.wrong_example: This option is more better for me.
language.better_example: This option is better for me.
language.reason: Better already carries the comparative meaning.

language.problem: Overusing very or really with basic adjectives
language.wrong_example: It was very good and really important for me.
language.better_example: It turned out to be more meaningful than I expected.
language.reason: A precise evaluation sounds more natural than stacking intensifiers on basic adjectives.

language.problem: Repeating I think in every sentence
language.wrong_example: I think it is useful, and I think it matters because I think it affects daily life.
language.better_example: What makes it useful to me is the way it affects daily life.
language.reason: Removing repeated I think makes the answer sound more confident and more fluent.

core.example: I would naturally use "viewpoint" when talking about this topic.
I would naturally use "comparison" when talking about this topic.
I would naturally use "difference" when talking about this topic.
I would naturally use "condition" when talking about this topic.
I would naturally use "reason" when talking about this topic.
I would naturally use "context" when talking about this topic.

extended.note: Useful when your answer depends on conditions.
Useful when you want to avoid sounding rigid.

core.example: I would use "from my point of view" naturally in this answer.
I would use "it depends on" naturally in this answer.
I would use "on the one hand" naturally in this answer.
I would use "on the other hand" naturally in this answer.
I would use "I partly agree because" naturally in this answer.
I would use "the main difference is that" naturally in this answer.

extended.phrase: I would not make it absolute
extended.meaning_zh: 我不会把它说成绝对的
extended.example: I would use "I would not make it absolute" when making the answer sound more natural.

extended.phrase: it works better in some cases than others
extended.meaning_zh: 它在某些情况下更有效
extended.example: I would use "it works better in some cases than others" when making the answer sound more natural.

extended.phrase: the stronger point for me is
extended.meaning_zh: 对我来说更有力的一点是
extended.example: I would use "the stronger point for me is" when making the answer sound more natural.

extended.phrase: I can still see the other side
extended.example: I would use "I can still see the other side" when making the answer sound more natural.

framework.goal: Express a view through position, reason, contrast, and condition.

moves: State your view or preference directly.
Add one quick sentence to frame the topic.
Explain your main reason and compare it with the other side.
Use one clear criterion to keep the answer organised.
End with a condition, limit, or flexible conclusion.
Avoid sounding absolute if the topic is context-dependent.

delivery_markers: That said, I can see why ...

samples.answer:
My response to an idea that is partly right but not complete is something I can talk about quite naturally. If someone says that people are more productive only when they are under pressure, I would say I partly agree rather than fully accept that idea. A small amount of pressure can help people focus, especially when a deadline creates urgency, but too much pressure usually reduces quality and clear thinking. I have seen people work efficiently under short-term pressure, but I have also seen the same pressure lead to rushed decisions and avoidable mistakes. That is why I would not reject the idea completely, but I would definitely limit it.

What made it worth talking about was not only the event or idea itself, but also the effect it had on the way I think or act. To me, the effect of pressure depends on the amount, the person, and how long it lasts. Overall, it is a topic that feels real to me because it connects to ordinary life rather than a perfect story.
highlights: Band 6 answer matched to the 同意、反对或部分同意 topic
Keeps spoken rhythm while staying specific and teachable

opening: The topic that comes to mind is my response to an idea that is partly right but not complete.
I would first explain the background clearly.
body: Then I would add the central detail or turning point.
After that, I would show the result or practical effect.
closing: Finally, I would end with the takeaway or present view.

upgrades.improved: To me, the effect of pressure depends on the amount, the person, and how long it lasts.
upgrades.why: A specific reason sounds much more natural than a general claim.
upgrades.note: Use the personal effect to close the answer.

upgrades.improved: I have seen people work efficiently under short-term pressure, but I have also seen the same pressure lead to rushed decisions and avoidable mistakes.
upgrades.why: A clear turning point gives the answer shape and movement.
upgrades.note: This is especially useful in spoken storytelling and reflective answers.

upgrades.original: It was very important to me.
upgrades.improved: What made it important was the effect it had on the way I handled similar situations later.
upgrades.why: This explains importance through a concrete effect instead of a vague adjective.
upgrades.note: Useful when you want a simple but natural reflective sentence.

upgrades.original: I learned a lot from it.
upgrades.improved: I came away from it with a clearer idea of what I would do next time.
upgrades.why: This sounds more spoken and more specific than a flat learned a lot.
upgrades.note: Good for endings that focus on takeaway and future behaviour.

samples.answer:
The topic that comes to mind for me is my response to an idea that is partly right but not complete. If someone says that people are more productive only when they are under pressure, I would say I partly agree rather than fully accept that idea. A small amount of pressure can help people focus, especially when a deadline creates urgency, but too much pressure usually reduces quality and clear thinking. What stood out most was that i have seen people work efficiently under short-term pressure, but I have also seen the same pressure lead to rushed decisions and avoidable mistakes.

After that, that is why I would not reject the idea completely, but I would definitely limit it. What I find interesting about it now is that the situation seemed fairly ordinary at the time, but it revealed something more lasting about how I handle pressure, choices, or relationships. To me, the effect of pressure depends on the amount, the person, and how long it lasts. So if I were explaining this in a speaking test, I would focus less on sounding dramatic and more on why the experience or idea genuinely stayed with me.
highlights: Band 7 answer matched to the 同意、反对或部分同意 topic
Keeps spoken rhythm while staying specific and teachable

opening: The topic that comes to mind is my response to an idea that is partly right but not complete.
I would first explain the background clearly.
body: Then I would add the central detail or turning point.
After that, I would show the result or practical effect.
closing: Finally, I would end with the takeaway or present view.

upgrades.improved: To me, the effect of pressure depends on the amount, the person, and how long it lasts.
upgrades.why: A specific reason sounds much more natural than a general claim.
upgrades.note: Use the personal effect to close the answer.

upgrades.improved: I have seen people work efficiently under short-term pressure, but I have also seen the same pressure lead to rushed decisions and avoidable mistakes.
upgrades.why: A clear turning point gives the answer shape and movement.
upgrades.note: This is especially useful in spoken storytelling and reflective answers.

upgrades.improved: What helped most was that it changed the way I judged the situation in practice.
upgrades.why: This sounds more mature and more natural than the vague phrase helped me a lot.
upgrades.note: Useful when you want to show growth without sounding dramatic.

upgrades.improved: It gave me a more balanced way to look at similar situations afterwards.
upgrades.why: Balanced way to look at is a more idiomatic reflective phrase in spoken English.
upgrades.note: Works well in opinion, process, and experience answers.

samples.answer:
If I had to answer this topic in a more reflective way, I would still begin with my response to an idea that is partly right but not complete. If someone says that people are more productive only when they are under pressure, I would say I partly agree rather than fully accept that idea. A small amount of pressure can help people focus, especially when a deadline creates urgency, but too much pressure usually reduces quality and clear thinking. The turning point, for me, was that i have seen people work efficiently under short-term pressure, but I have also seen the same pressure lead to rushed decisions and avoidable mistakes.

From there, that is why I would not reject the idea completely, but I would definitely limit it. What matters now is not the surface event or statement alone, but the shift in perspective behind it. To me, the effect of pressure depends on the amount, the person, and how long it lasts. In that sense, the topic feels useful because it shows not only what happened or what I think, but also how my judgment has become a little more precise over time.
highlights: Band 8 answer matched to the 同意、反对或部分同意 topic
Keeps spoken rhythm while staying specific and teachable

opening: The topic that comes to mind is my response to an idea that is partly right but not complete.
I would first explain the background clearly.
body: Then I would add the central detail or turning point.
After that, I would show the result or practical effect.
closing: Finally, I would end with the takeaway or present view.

upgrades.improved: To me, the effect of pressure depends on the amount, the person, and how long it lasts.
upgrades.why: A specific reason sounds much more natural than a general claim.
upgrades.note: Use the personal effect to close the answer.

upgrades.improved: I have seen people work efficiently under short-term pressure, but I have also seen the same pressure lead to rushed decisions and avoidable mistakes.
upgrades.why: A clear turning point gives the answer shape and movement.
upgrades.note: This is especially useful in spoken storytelling and reflective answers.

upgrades.improved: What stayed with me was not the event itself, but the shift it created in my judgment afterwards.
upgrades.why: This upgrade sounds more nuanced and more reflective than a broad claim like it changed me.
upgrades.note: Strong for higher-band closings that need subtlety rather than drama.

upgrades.improved: Its real value lay in the way it quietly reshaped my response patterns over time.
upgrades.why: Quietly reshaped and over time make the reflection sound more precise and more natural.
upgrades.note: Useful when you want a sophisticated closing without sounding academic.

practice.task: Explain whether you agree, disagree, or partly agree with an idea.
checklist: Compare with at least one clear criterion.
Use a flexible ending if the topic depends on context.
self_prompts: What can the other side offer?
